metadata.description: Standard normal examination results for infants.

results.male_result: Inspection: Abdomen is normally protuberant and rounded (pot-bellied) due to weak abdominal musculature; respiration is diaphragmatic (abdominal movement is prominent); umbilicus is clean, without redness or discharge; a small umbilical hernia may be present; diastasis recti may be visible as a midline ridge. Auscultation: Bowel sounds are active; metallic tinkling sounds are often heard. Percussion: Generalized tympany is common due to air swallowing; dullness over liver. Palpation: Abdomen is soft and non-tender (infant appears comfortable); liver edge is commonly palpable 1-3 cm below the right costal margin; spleen tip may be palpable 1-2 cm below the left costal margin; lower poles of kidneys are frequently palpable; no abnormal masses; no inguinal hernias.
results.female_result: Inspection: Abdomen is normally protuberant and rounded (pot-bellied) due to weak abdominal musculature; respiration is diaphragmatic (abdominal movement is prominent); umbilicus is clean, without redness or discharge; a small umbilical hernia may be present; diastasis recti may be visible as a midline ridge. Auscultation: Bowel sounds are active; metallic tinkling sounds are often heard. Percussion: Generalized tympany is common due to air swallowing; dullness over liver. Palpation: Abdomen is soft and non-tender (infant appears comfortable); liver edge is commonly palpable 1-3 cm below the right costal margin; spleen tip may be palpable 1-2 cm below the left costal margin; lower poles of kidneys are frequently palpable; no abnormal masses; no inguinal hernias.

results.male_result: Not applicable / Contraindicated - The Abdominal Thrust Maneuver is generally not performed on infants (under 1 year) due to the risk of injury to abdominal organs. In a healthy male infant, the airway is patent, and breathing is effortless and noiseless.
results.female_result: Not applicable / Contraindicated - The Abdominal Thrust Maneuver is generally not performed on infants (under 1 year) due to the risk of injury to abdominal organs. In a healthy female infant, the airway is patent, and breathing is effortless and noiseless.

results.male_result: Negative. Although infants naturally exhibit higher physiological ligamentous laxity compared to adults, the knee should demonstrate mechanical stability. There is no pathological anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur, and a firm endpoint is palpable. The degree of translation must be symmetric when compared to the contralateral knee.
results.female_result: Negative. Although infants naturally exhibit higher physiological ligamentous laxity compared to adults, the knee should demonstrate mechanical stability. There is no pathological anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur, and a firm endpoint is palpable. The degree of translation must be symmetric when compared to the contralateral knee.

results.male_result: Nasal vestibule is intact. Nasal septum is generally midline. Nasal mucosa appears pink, smooth, and moist. Nasal passages are narrow compared to adults but patent bilaterally. Inferior turbinates are visible and normal in color. No nasal flaring, discharge, bleeding, masses, or foreign bodies observed.
results.female_result: Nasal vestibule is intact. Nasal septum is generally midline. Nasal mucosa appears pink, smooth, and moist. Nasal passages are narrow compared to adults but patent bilaterally. Inferior turbinates are visible and normal in color. No nasal flaring, discharge, bleeding, masses, or foreign bodies observed.

results.male_result: At birth: Weight 2.5-4.5 kg, Length 46-54 cm, Head Circumference 33-36 cm. At 6 months: Weight 6.4-9.8 kg, Length 63-72 cm. At 12 months: Weight 7.8-11.8 kg, Length 71-80 cm, Head Circumference approx. 46 cm. (Values vary by percentile)
results.female_result: At birth: Weight 2.4-4.2 kg, Length 45-53 cm, Head Circumference 32-35 cm. At 6 months: Weight 5.8-9.2 kg, Length 61-70 cm. At 12 months: Weight 7.0-11.5 kg, Length 69-79 cm, Head Circumference approx. 45 cm. (Values vary by percentile)

results.male_result: Normal score: 7-10. Scores 4-6 indicate moderate depression/difficulty; scores 0-3 indicate severe depression requiring immediate resuscitation.
results.female_result: Normal score: 7-10. Scores 4-6 indicate moderate depression/difficulty; scores 0-3 indicate severe depression requiring immediate resuscitation.

results.male_result: Negative - No palpable lymph nodes at the medial aspect of the inguinal ligament (Cloquet's node area). The region is soft, non-tender, and free of discrete nodular masses upon gentle deep palpation.
results.female_result: Negative - No palpable lymph nodes at the medial aspect of the inguinal ligament (Cloquet's node area). The region is soft, non-tender, and free of discrete nodular masses upon gentle deep palpation.

results.male_result: Negative. Assessment is based on observation of spontaneous movement and reactions to passive motion rather than verbal feedback. Normal findings include symmetrical spontaneous movement of the upper extremities and a symmetrical Moro reflex. Gentle passive abduction and extension of the arm should not elicit signs of distress, crying, grimacing, or guarding.
results.female_result: Negative. Assessment is based on observation of spontaneous movement and reactions to passive motion rather than verbal feedback. Normal findings include symmetrical spontaneous movement of the upper extremities and a symmetrical Moro reflex. Gentle passive abduction and extension of the arm should not elicit signs of distress, crying, grimacing, or guarding.

results.male_result: Chest wall is symmetric. Mild, bilateral breast enlargement (palpable breast buds) may be present due to maternal estrogen transfer, which is a normal physiological finding. Nipples are symmetric and intact. Transitory milky discharge ('witch's milk') may occur. No erythema, warmth, induration, or signs of neonatal mastitis.
results.female_result: Chest wall is symmetric. Mild, bilateral breast enlargement (palpable breast buds) is common due to maternal estrogen transfer and is considered normal. Nipples are symmetric and intact. Transitory milky discharge ('witch's milk') may occur. No erythema, warmth, induration, or signs of neonatal mastitis. No supernumerary nipples visible along the milk line.

results.male_result: Negative: Less than 10 petechiae per 5 cm diameter circle on the volar surface of the forearm (or appropriate site based on infant size) after 5 minutes of venous occlusion. The cuff pressure must be set midway between the infant's specific systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Skin appears normal without ecchymoses.
results.female_result: Negative: Less than 10 petechiae per 5 cm diameter circle on the volar surface of the forearm (or appropriate site based on infant size) after 5 minutes of venous occlusion. The cuff pressure must be set midway between the infant's specific systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Skin appears normal without ecchymoses.

results.male_result: Not applicable/Not routinely performed. Infants cannot cooperate with instructions for maximal inspiration and expiration.
results.female_result: Not applicable/Not routinely performed. Infants cannot cooperate with instructions for maximal inspiration and expiration.

results.male_result: Visual acuity: Assessed via fixation behavior (Central, Steady, Maintained - CSM) and ability to fixate and follow (F&F) a light or object. Quantitative equivalent is approx. 20/400 at birth, improving to 20/50-20/60 by 12 months. Red Reflex: Bright, equal, and symmetrical in both eyes (negative for leukocoria/white pupil). Pupils: Round, reactive to light, typically smaller (miotic) than adults. Extraocular movements: Full range of motion; intermittent misalignment (strabismus) is normal up to 3-4 months; eyes should be aligned thereafter. Anterior segment: Cornea clear (horizontal diameter ~10mm, smaller than adult). Intraocular pressure: 10-18 mmHg (typically lower than adults). Fundus: Optic disc margins defined (may appear paler); macula reflex continues to develop.
results.female_result: Visual acuity: Assessed via fixation behavior (Central, Steady, Maintained - CSM) and ability to fixate and follow (F&F) a light or object. Quantitative equivalent is approx. 20/400 at birth, improving to 20/50-20/60 by 12 months. Red Reflex: Bright, equal, and symmetrical in both eyes (negative for leukocoria/white pupil). Pupils: Round, reactive to light, typically smaller (miotic) than adults. Extraocular movements: Full range of motion; intermittent misalignment (strabismus) is normal up to 3-4 months; eyes should be aligned thereafter. Anterior segment: Cornea clear (horizontal diameter ~10mm, smaller than adult). Intraocular pressure: 10-18 mmHg (typically lower than adults). Fundus: Optic disc margins defined (may appear paler); macula reflex continues to develop.

results.male_result: Cornea is clear, transparent, and moist. The corneal surface is smooth. Horizontal corneal diameter is appropriate for age (typically smaller than adults). No opacities, edema, haziness, or enlarged diameter (buphthalmos) observed. Corneal reflex is present. No dermoids or vascularization.
results.female_result: Cornea is clear, transparent, and moist. The corneal surface is smooth. Horizontal corneal diameter is appropriate for age (typically smaller than adults). No opacities, edema, haziness, or enlarged diameter (buphthalmos) observed. Corneal reflex is present. No dermoids or vascularization.

results.male_result: In early infancy (0-3 months), intermittent exotropia or variable alignment may be observed as a normal physiological finding. By 4-6 months, ocular alignment should stabilize to orthophoria (no movement of the uncovered eye). Fixation should be central, steady, and maintained on an age-appropriate target (e.g., light or toy).
results.female_result: In early infancy (0-3 months), intermittent exotropia or variable alignment may be observed as a normal physiological finding. By 4-6 months, ocular alignment should stabilize to orthophoria (no movement of the uncovered eye). Fixation should be central, steady, and maintained on an age-appropriate target (e.g., light or toy).

results.male_result: Normal skin architecture consistent with infancy; the pigment network is often faint, delicate, or absent in the background skin due to a flatter dermo-epidermal junction and lower pigmentation. The background color may appear pinker with visible regular vascular structures (regular dotted vessels) due to a thinner epidermis. Acquired melanocytic nevi are rare; if small congenital nevi are present, they typically exhibit a globular or cobblestone pattern rather than the reticular network seen in adults. Physiological findings such as milia or sebaceous hyperplasia on the face may be present.
results.female_result: Normal skin architecture consistent with infancy; the pigment network is often faint, delicate, or absent in the background skin due to a flatter dermo-epidermal junction and lower pigmentation. The background color may appear pinker with visible regular vascular structures (regular dotted vessels) due to a thinner epidermis. Acquired melanocytic nevi are rare; if small congenital nevi are present, they typically exhibit a globular or cobblestone pattern rather than the reticular network seen in adults. Physiological findings such as milia or sebaceous hyperplasia on the face may be present.

results.male_result: The anal opening is patent and properly positioned. Anal sphincter tone is normal, with a positive anal wink reflex elicited upon stimulation. The rectal mucosa is smooth. The prostate is rudimentary, extremely small, and typically not palpable. No palpable masses, polyps, or tenderness. No blood or abnormal discharge on the examining finger.
results.female_result: The anal opening is patent and properly positioned relative to the vaginal vestibule. Anal sphincter tone is normal, with a positive anal wink reflex elicited upon stimulation. The rectal mucosa is smooth without palpable masses or tenderness. No blood or abnormal discharge on the examining finger.

results.male_result: Negative test indicated by structural stability of the medial ankle. While infants exhibit natural physiological hypermobility and greater range of motion compared to adults, there should be a distinct end-point (end-feel) without gross instability or excessive widening of the medial joint space. No signs of distress, withdrawal, or crying upon manipulation compared to the contralateral side.
results.female_result: Negative test indicated by structural stability of the medial ankle. While infants exhibit natural physiological hypermobility and greater range of motion compared to adults, there should be a distinct end-point (end-feel) without gross instability or excessive widening of the medial joint space. No signs of distress, withdrawal, or crying upon manipulation compared to the contralateral side.

results.male_result: 8-14 mm; difference between both eyes ≤2 mm. Values increase with age as the orbit and globe develop. Eyes should be symmetrically positioned.
results.female_result: 8-14 mm; difference between both eyes ≤2 mm. Values increase with age as the orbit and globe develop. Eyes should be symmetrically positioned.

results.male_result: Eyelids: No ptosis, edema, or lesions; epicanthal folds may be present (pseudostrabismus). Conjunctiva: Clear and pink. Sclera: White, may have a slight bluish tint in newborns due to thinness. Cornea: Clear, positive red reflex bilaterally. Pupils: Equal, round, reactive to light; generally smaller (miotic) than adults. Iris: Intact, color may not be permanent. Lacrimal apparatus: No discharge or inflammation; tear production is often minimal or absent in the first 1-3 months. Extraocular movements: Eyes appear symmetrical; intermittent strabismus (crossing) is normal up to 3-4 months of age; infant demonstrates ability to fixate and follow light or objects (tracking) appropriate for developmental age.
results.female_result: Eyelids: No ptosis, edema, or lesions; epicanthal folds may be present (pseudostrabismus). Conjunctiva: Clear and pink. Sclera: White, may have a slight bluish tint in newborns due to thinness. Cornea: Clear, positive red reflex bilaterally. Pupils: Equal, round, reactive to light; generally smaller (miotic) than adults. Iris: Intact, color may not be permanent. Lacrimal apparatus: No discharge or inflammation; tear production is often minimal or absent in the first 1-3 months. Extraocular movements: Eyes appear symmetrical; intermittent strabismus (crossing) is normal up to 3-4 months of age; infant demonstrates ability to fixate and follow light or objects (tracking) appropriate for developmental age.

results.male_result: Penis: Normal appearance for age (typically 2-3 cm length), urethral meatus located at the tip of the glans (no hypospadias or epispadias). Foreskin: Often tight and non-retractable (physiologic phimosis is normal in infants); do not forcibly retract. Scrotum: Rugated and symmetrical. Testes: Descended and palpable in the scrotum bilaterally, small size (approx. 1-2 cm or < 2 ml volume). Hydroceles (fluid collection) are common and may transilluminate. Inguinal region: No bulge suggestive of hernia.
results.female_result: Vulva: No pubic hair (Tanner stage I). Labia majora may appear swollen and pigmented due to maternal estrogen effects; labia minora present. Clitoris: Normal size, though may appear relatively prominent. Urethral meatus: Visible below the clitoris. Vaginal introitus: Hymen is typically annular and prominent. Discharge: White mucoid discharge or slight bloody withdrawal bleeding (pseudomenses) is normal in neonates due to maternal hormone withdrawal. Inguinal region: No hernias or lymphadenopathy.

results.male_result: Eyelids are symmetrical bilaterally. Epicanthal folds (vertical skin folds covering the medial canthus) may be present and are considered a normal variation, often causing the appearance of pseudostrabismus. No congenital ptosis, ectropion, or entropion. Eyelid margins are smooth. No edema, erythema, hemangiomas, or purulent discharge. Eyelashes are present, though may be fine. No palpable masses (e.g., dermoid cysts). Palpebral fissures are proportionate to facial size (smaller dimensions than adults). Spontaneous blinking is observed, and lid closure is complete during sleep.
results.female_result: Eyelids are symmetrical bilaterally. Epicanthal folds (vertical skin folds covering the medial canthus) may be present and are considered a normal variation, often causing the appearance of pseudostrabismus. No congenital ptosis, ectropion, or entropion. Eyelid margins are smooth. No edema, erythema, hemangiomas, or purulent discharge. Eyelashes are present, though may be fine. No palpable masses (e.g., dermoid cysts). Palpebral fissures are proportionate to facial size (smaller dimensions than adults). Spontaneous blinking is observed, and lid closure is complete during sleep.

results.male_result: Negative. The infant tolerates passive hip extension with the knee flexed without exhibiting signs of pain or distress. There is no grimacing, crying, or reflexive withdrawal of the limb. Symmetrical movement and reaction are observed bilaterally.
results.female_result: Negative. The infant tolerates passive hip extension with the knee flexed without exhibiting signs of pain or distress. There is no grimacing, crying, or reflexive withdrawal of the limb. Symmetrical movement and reaction are observed bilaterally.

results.male_result: Not applicable
results.female_result: Not applicable. Normal infants score 0 as they lack terminal hair in androgen-sensitive areas.

results.male_result: Not applicable
results.female_result: Not applicable

results.male_result: Not applicable. Infants cannot stand independently or follow the specific instructions required for this test.
results.female_result: Not applicable. Infants cannot stand independently or follow the specific instructions required for this test.

results.male_result: Open angle configuration. Anterior chamber angle structures (Schwalbe's line, trabecular meshwork, and scleral spur) are visible. The trabecular meshwork typically appears translucent and lacks significant pigmentation. The iris insertion is flat and positioned posterior to the scleral spur. No peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS), abnormal membranes (such as Barkan's membrane), or neovascularization.
results.female_result: Open angle configuration. Anterior chamber angle structures (Schwalbe's line, trabecular meshwork, and scleral spur) are visible. The trabecular meshwork typically appears translucent and lacks significant pigmentation. The iris insertion is flat and positioned posterior to the scleral spur. No peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS), abnormal membranes (such as Barkan's membrane), or neovascularization.

results.male_result: In infants, normal hip abduction is significantly greater than in adults due to ligamentous laxity. With the infant supine and the hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, the hips should abduct easily to approximately 70-90 degrees (often allowing the lateral thigh to nearly touch the examination table). Movement must be symmetrical, smooth, and free of palpable 'clunks' or clicks (negative Ortolani and Barlow signs). Limitation of abduction (specifically less than 60 degrees) or asymmetry is considered abnormal.
results.female_result: Normal hip abduction in female infants ranges from 70-90 degrees with the hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees. The movement should be effortless and symmetrical, allowing the thighs to fall outward toward the bed (frog-leg position). Since females are at a statistically higher risk for Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH), careful assessment for asymmetry or limitation (< 60 degrees) is critical. The exam should be painless with no joint instability noted.

results.male_result: Nasopharyngeal mucosa is pink and moist. Adenoid tissue (pharyngeal tonsil) is visible on the posterior superior wall; its presence is a normal physiological finding in infants due to immune system development, provided it does not completely obstruct the choanae. Eustachian tube orifices are patent, though anatomical orientation is more horizontal compared to adults. No purulent secretions, polyps, or foreign bodies are observed.
results.female_result: Nasopharyngeal mucosa is pink and moist. Adenoid tissue (pharyngeal tonsil) is visible on the posterior superior wall; its presence is a normal physiological finding in infants due to immune system development, provided it does not completely obstruct the choanae. Eustachian tube orifices are patent, though anatomical orientation is more horizontal compared to adults. No purulent secretions, polyps, or foreign bodies are observed.

results.male_result: Mental status: Alert, responsive to environment and caregiver (e.g., social smile, eye contact); fixes and follows visual objects (age-dependent). Cranial nerves: Pupils equal and reactive to light; blinks to visual threat (if >2-3 months); symmetric facial movements during crying and smiling; strong suck and swallow reflexes; turns head toward sound. Motor: Normal muscle tone (predominantly flexor tone in early infancy) and bulk; spontaneous, symmetric movements of all extremities; no hypotonia (floppiness) or hypertonia; appropriate head control for age (e.g., no head lag when pulled to sit if >4 months). Sensory: Withdraws limb or cries in response to tactile or painful stimuli. Reflexes: Deep tendon reflexes present and symmetric (may be brisk/3+ compared to adults); Primitive reflexes (Moro, palmar/plantar grasp, rooting, tonic neck) present and appropriate for age. Plantar response is extensor (positive Babinski) bilaterally, which is normal. Coordination: Assessed via developmental milestones (reaching, grasping, rolling, sitting).
results.female_result: Mental status: Alert, responsive to environment and caregiver (e.g., social smile, eye contact); fixes and follows visual objects (age-dependent). Cranial nerves: Pupils equal and reactive to light; blinks to visual threat (if >2-3 months); symmetric facial movements during crying and smiling; strong suck and swallow reflexes; turns head toward sound. Motor: Normal muscle tone (predominantly flexor tone in early infancy) and bulk; spontaneous, symmetric movements of all extremities; no hypotonia (floppiness) or hypertonia; appropriate head control for age (e.g., no head lag when pulled to sit if >4 months). Sensory: Withdraws limb or cries in response to tactile or painful stimuli. Reflexes: Deep tendon reflexes present and symmetric (may be brisk/3+ compared to adults); Primitive reflexes (Moro, palmar/plantar grasp, rooting, tonic neck) present and appropriate for age. Plantar response is extensor (positive Babinski) bilaterally, which is normal. Coordination: Assessed via developmental milestones (reaching, grasping, rolling, sitting).

results.male_result: Not applicable (This test requires the patient to stand independently)
results.female_result: Not applicable (This test requires the patient to stand independently)

results.male_result: In early infancy (0-3 months), eye movements may be saccadic (jerky) and transient intermittent strabismus (misalignment) is considered normal. By 3-4 months, the infant should demonstrate stable fixation and smooth pursuit, following a face or light source. Full range of motion is assessed via spontaneous tracking or the vestibulo-ocular reflex (Doll's head maneuver). By 6 months, eyes should be consistently aligned (orthophoric) and move conjugately. No pathologic nystagmus.
results.female_result: In early infancy (0-3 months), eye movements may be saccadic (jerky) and transient intermittent strabismus (misalignment) is considered normal. By 3-4 months, the infant should demonstrate stable fixation and smooth pursuit, following a face or light source. Full range of motion is assessed via spontaneous tracking or the vestibulo-ocular reflex (Doll's head maneuver). By 6 months, eyes should be consistently aligned (orthophoric) and move conjugately. No pathologic nystagmus.

results.male_result: Red reflex is present, bright, and symmetric bilaterally (crucial for ruling out congenital cataracts and retinoblastoma). The optic disc may appear paler (greyish-white to pale pink) compared to adults, which is a normal developmental finding in infants. The physiologic cup is usually very small or absent. Retinal vessels are normal in caliber and course, extending to the periphery. The macula is immature; the foveal light reflex is often dull or absent, and the macular region is less distinct or pigmented than in adults. No hemorrhages, exudates, or abnormal pigmentary changes.
results.female_result: Red reflex is present, bright, and symmetric bilaterally (crucial for ruling out congenital cataracts and retinoblastoma). The optic disc may appear paler (greyish-white to pale pink) compared to adults, which is a normal developmental finding in infants. The physiologic cup is usually very small or absent. Retinal vessels are normal in caliber and course, extending to the periphery. The macula is immature; the foveal light reflex is often dull or absent, and the macular region is less distinct or pigmented than in adults. No hemorrhages, exudates, or abnormal pigmentary changes.

results.male_result: Lips: Pink, moist, intact; sucking calluses (tubercles) may be present on the upper lip. Oral mucosa: Pink, moist, intact. Gingiva: Pink, firm, smooth ridges. Teeth: Generally edentulous (no teeth) at birth and early infancy; eruption of deciduous mandibular central incisors typically begins around 6-10 months. Tongue: Pink, moist, fits within the floor of the mouth, moves freely (no ankyloglossia/tongue-tie). Hard Palate: Intact (no clefts); Epstein's pearls (small whitish-yellow cysts) are a common normal variant. Soft Palate: Intact, symmetric elevation. Uvula: Midline. Tonsils: Usually small or barely visible in early infancy. Pharynx: Pink, no erythema. Saliva: Drooling is common and normal starting around 3 months of age.
results.female_result: Lips: Pink, moist, intact; sucking calluses (tubercles) may be present on the upper lip. Oral mucosa: Pink, moist, intact. Gingiva: Pink, firm, smooth ridges. Teeth: Generally edentulous (no teeth) at birth and early infancy; eruption of deciduous mandibular central incisors typically begins around 6-10 months. Tongue: Pink, moist, fits within the floor of the mouth, moves freely (no ankyloglossia/tongue-tie). Hard Palate: Intact (no clefts); Epstein's pearls (small whitish-yellow cysts) are a common normal variant. Soft Palate: Intact, symmetric elevation. Uvula: Midline. Tonsils: Usually small or barely visible in early infancy. Pharynx: Pink, no erythema. Saliva: Drooling is common and normal starting around 3 months of age.

results.male_result: Oral mucosa is pink, moist, and intact. The oral cavity is typically edentulous (no teeth) in early infancy; deciduous teeth usually begin to erupt around 6 months of age (starting with lower central incisors). Tongue is midline, pink, and mobile without ankyloglossia (tongue-tie). Hard and soft palates are intact with normal arching; Epstein's pearls (small white/yellow cysts) may be present on the palate or gums and are a normal finding. Tonsils are generally small or barely visible. Posterior pharyngeal wall is pink without erythema or exudate. Drooling is common and normal after 3 months. Gag reflex is present and often strong.
results.female_result: Oral mucosa is pink, moist, and intact. The oral cavity is typically edentulous (no teeth) in early infancy; deciduous teeth usually begin to erupt around 6 months of age (starting with lower central incisors). Tongue is midline, pink, and mobile without ankyloglossia (tongue-tie). Hard and soft palates are intact with normal arching; Epstein's pearls (small white/yellow cysts) may be present on the palate or gums and are a normal finding. Tonsils are generally small or barely visible. Posterior pharyngeal wall is pink without erythema or exudate. Drooling is common and normal after 3 months. Gag reflex is present and often strong.

results.male_result: Not applicable. Standard orthostatic testing requires the patient to stand independently, which is developmentally impossible for infants. For reference, normal resting supine vital signs for infants (1-12 months) are: Heart Rate 100-160 bpm; Systolic Blood Pressure 70-105 mmHg (increases with age within infancy).
results.female_result: Not applicable. Standard orthostatic testing requires the patient to stand independently, which is developmentally impossible for infants. For reference, normal resting supine vital signs for infants (1-12 months) are: Heart Rate 100-160 bpm; Systolic Blood Pressure 70-105 mmHg (increases with age within infancy).

results.male_result: External auditory canal is patent; the canal is shorter and the bony portion is not yet fully developed. Tympanic membrane is intact, pearly gray, and translucent. The membrane is oriented more horizontally (oblique) compared to adults, making it appear to lie almost flat against the canal floor. Landmarks (malleus handle) are visible. The light reflex is present but may appear slightly different in shape due to the membrane's angle. Pneumatic otoscopy demonstrates normal mobility of the membrane. No signs of bulging, retraction, perforation, or middle ear effusion.
results.female_result: External auditory canal is patent; the canal is shorter and the bony portion is not yet fully developed. Tympanic membrane is intact, pearly gray, and translucent. The membrane is oriented more horizontally (oblique) compared to adults, making it appear to lie almost flat against the canal floor. Landmarks (malleus handle) are visible. The light reflex is present but may appear slightly different in shape due to the membrane's angle. Pneumatic otoscopy demonstrates normal mobility of the membrane. No signs of bulging, retraction, perforation, or middle ear effusion.

results.male_result: Not applicable. This test is not clinically indicated or feasible for infants. It requires the patient to voluntarily contract the quadriceps muscle upon command, which an infant cannot do. Additionally, the infant cannot verbally report specific pain associated with the maneuver.
results.female_result: Not applicable. This test is not clinically indicated or feasible for infants. It requires the patient to voluntarily contract the quadriceps muscle upon command, which an infant cannot do. Additionally, the infant cannot verbally report specific pain associated with the maneuver.

results.male_result: Negative. With the infant supine and the knee gently extended (stabilizing against physiological flexion), compression of the suprapatellar pouch followed by gentle pressure on the patella produces no palpable click or floating sensation. The patella remains seated against the femoral condyles with no ballottement detected, indicating absence of significant knee joint effusion.
results.female_result: Negative. With the infant supine and the knee gently extended (stabilizing against physiological flexion), compression of the suprapatellar pouch followed by gentle pressure on the patella produces no palpable click or floating sensation. The patella remains seated against the femoral condyles with no ballottement detected, indicating absence of significant knee joint effusion.

results.male_result: Negative. The infant lies supine. The examiner passively places the tested leg into a position of flexion, abduction, and external rotation. Due to the natural ligamentous laxity and physiological flexibility characteristic of infancy, the knee should easily descend to the level of the examination table without resistance. The infant remains calm and shows no signs of distress, guarding, or pain (such as crying or grimacing) during the passive movement.
results.female_result: Negative. The infant lies supine. The examiner passively places the tested leg into a position of flexion, abduction, and external rotation. Due to the natural ligamentous laxity and physiological flexibility characteristic of infancy, the knee should easily descend to the level of the examination table without resistance. The infant remains calm and shows no signs of distress, guarding, or pain (such as crying or grimacing) during the passive movement.

results.male_result: Negative. No signs of pelvic instability, crepitus, or abnormal movement detected. No objective signs of pain or distress (such as crying, grimacing, or withdrawal) elicited upon very gentle bilateral compression of the iliac wings or distraction of the iliac spines. The pelvis feels stable.
results.female_result: Negative. No signs of pelvic instability, crepitus, or abnormal movement detected. No objective signs of pain or distress (such as crying, grimacing, or withdrawal) elicited upon very gentle bilateral compression of the iliac wings or distraction of the iliac spines. The pelvis feels stable.

results.male_result: External genitalia inspection: Penis is normal in appearance with the urethral meatus at the tip; foreskin is typically non-retractable (physiologic phimosis). Scrotum is rugated and pigmented. Testes are palpable and descended bilaterally in the scrotum. Transitory hydrocele may be present.
results.female_result: External genitalia inspection only (internal/bimanual exam is contra-indicated). Labia majora typically cover the labia minora and clitoris in full-term infants. Mild vulvar edema, milky white discharge (physiologic leukorrhea), or slight withdrawal bleeding (pseudomenses) may be present due to maternal estrogen effects. The hymen is intact and visible.

results.male_result: For full-term newborns and infants, the mean stretched penile length is approximately 3.5 cm (ranging roughly 2.8 to 4.2 cm). A stretched penile length of less than 1.9 cm (typically defined as < 2.5 SD below the mean) is considered micropenis. The foreskin is almost always present and is usually non-retractable (physiologic phimosis) and adherent to the glans, which is a normal finding for this age. The urethral meatus should be located at the tip of the glans.
results.female_result: Not applicable

results.male_result: General appearance: Active, alert, well-nourished, consolable, no acute distress. Vital signs: Heart rate 100-160 bpm, Respiratory rate 30-53 breaths/min, Blood pressure (systolic 72-104 mmHg / diastolic 37-56 mmHg), Temperature 36.5-37.5°C (axillary or rectal). Growth: Weight, length, and head circumference tracking along appropriate percentiles on WHO growth charts. Head: Anterior fontanelle open, soft, and flat; posterior fontanelle may be closed or fingertip size; shape normal. Eyes: Red reflex present bilaterally, pupils equal and reactive, tracks faces or light. Ears: Tympanic membranes pearly gray, mobile, infant responds to sound. Nose/Throat: Nares patent, palate intact (no cleft), mucous membranes moist. Neck: Supple, no masses, full range of motion (no torticollis). Chest: Symmetric movement, clear breath sounds bilaterally. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no pathological murmurs, femoral pulses palpable and equal. Abdomen: Soft, rounded, non-tender, bowel sounds active, umbilical ring intact (or small reducible hernia). Musculoskeletal: Negative Ortolani and Barlow maneuvers (stable hips), spine straight without sacral dimples or tufts, symmetrical limb movement. Neurological: Appropriate primitive reflexes for age (e.g., Moro, grasp, rooting) or their integration; achieving age-appropriate developmental milestones (e.g., head control, rolling, sitting). Skin: Pink, intact, no jaundice, no significant rashes (e.g., diaper dermatitis). Genitourinary: Penis normal, urethral meatus at tip of glans (no hypospadias), testes descended bilaterally.

results.male_result: External auditory canal is patent. The tympanic membrane is intact and demonstrates distinct mobility upon pneumatic insufflation (positive and negative pressure). The membrane is typically pearly gray, although crying may cause it to appear pink or red due to vascular engorgement. The membrane is oriented more horizontally compared to adults, and the light reflex may appear diffuse. Landmarks such as the short process of the malleus are visible.

The lower poles of the kidneys may be palpable due to thin abdominal muscles. Femoral and brachial pulses are strong and symmetric. Testes are palpable and descended in the scrotum.

results.male_result: Pupils are typically smaller (physiological miosis) compared to adults, often ranging from 1.5 to 4 mm in diameter due to sympathetic nervous system immaturity. Direct and consensual light reflexes are present at birth (in term infants) but the amplitude of constriction may be smaller and harder to visualize due to the small baseline size. By 4-6 months, pupil size increases and reactivity resembles adult patterns. Accommodation reflex typically develops around 3-4 months.

results.male_result: Skin is soft, smooth, and intact with good elastic turgor (best assessed on the abdomen). results.male_result: Cornea: Clear and transparent, no fluorescein retention, punctate staining, or dendritic lesions indicating intact epithelium. Anterior chamber: Formed and quiet, though physiologically shallower than in adults. Lens: Clear, no congenital opacities. Eyelids: Normal position, no epiblepharon or trichiasis (lashes rubbing the cornea).

Passive lateral movement should meet no resistance and be symmetrical bilaterally.

results.male_result: In early infancy (0-3 months), intermittent eye deviation (particularly exotropia) may occur and is often transient/physiological. By 3-4 months, eyes should be consistently aligned (orthophoria) with symmetric corneal light reflexes (Hirschberg test). Pseudostrabismus (false appearance of esotropia due to a broad nasal bridge or epicanthal folds) is a common normal finding, confirmed by centered corneal light reflexes. The infant demonstrates Central, Steady, and Maintained (CSM) fixation on a target. No constant deviation should be present.

The infant demonstrates Central, Steady, and Maintained (CSM) fixation on a target. No constant deviation should be present.

results.male_result: Corneal light reflex is symmetric and centered on both pupils. Pseudostrabismus (false appearance of crossed eyes due to a broad nasal bridge or epicanthal folds) is common, but the symmetric light reflexes confirm normal ocular alignment (orthophoria).

results.male_result:
Values fluctuate due to 'minipuberty' (activation of HPG axis) typically between 1-6 months. 
Testosterone: 1-5 months: 10-250 ng/dL (0.3-8.7 nmol/L); 6-12 months: <7-20 ng/dL (Prepubertal levels). 
LH: 1-5 months: 0.8-5.0 mIU/mL; 6-12 months: <0.5 mIU/mL. 
FSH: 0.2-3.0 mIU/mL (variable). 
DHEA-S: High at birth due to fetal adrenal zone, declining rapidly to <50 μg/dL by 1 year.
results.female_result:
Values fluctuate due to 'minipuberty' (activation of HPG axis) but generally lower than adult females. 
Estradiol: 1-12 months: Generally <15 pg/mL (<55 pmol/L), transient elevations up to 40 pg/mL possible in first months. 
FSH: 1-12 months: 0.5-10.0 mIU/mL (Often higher than males in infancy). 
LH: <0.1-1.0 mIU/mL. 
Testosterone: <10 ng/dL. 
DHEA-S: High at birth, declining rapidly to <50 μg/dL by 1 year.

results.male_result: Results depend entirely on the specific analyte. For quantitative assays (e.g., hormones, immunoglobulins), values must be assessed against specific pediatric reference ranges.

results.male_result: HLA typing results are reported as specific allele designations for each locus. Normal finding: Successful identification of HLA alleles at Class I (HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C) and Class II (HLA-DR, HLA-DQ, HLA-DP) loci. Results expressed in standard nomenclature (e.g., HLA-A*02:01, HLA-B*07:02, HLA-DRB1*15:01). Each individual inherits one allele from each parent, resulting in heterozygous or homozygous expression at each locus. No 'normal' or 'abnormal' alleles exist; results represent individual genetic polymorphism.

results.male_result: Normal immunoelectrophoresis pattern consistent with infant physiology. IgG levels exhibit wide variation: high at birth (maternal transfer, 600-1400 mg/dL), dropping to a physiological nadir between 3-6 months (200-500 mg/dL), and slowly rising thereafter. IgA arcs are typically faint or barely visible (1-90 mg/dL) as synthesis is immature. IgM is present but lower than adult levels (20-120 mg/dL). IgD and IgE are often undetectable or very low. Normal polyclonal distribution with no monoclonal bands (M-protein). Kappa and lambda light chains present in normal ratio.
results.female_result: Normal immunoelectrophoresis pattern consistent with infant physiology. IgA arcs are typically faint or barely visible (1-90 mg/dL) as synthesis is immature. IgM is present but lower than adult levels (20-120 mg/dL). IgD and IgE are often undetectable or very low. Normal polyclonal distribution with no monoclonal bands (M-protein). Kappa and lambda light chains present in normal ratio.

results.male_result: No organisms seen in sterile body fluids (blood, CSF, pleural fluid, urine obtained via catheter or suprapubic aspiration). For non-sterile sites: Normal commensal flora consistent with specimen site. Respiratory samples (typically nasopharyngeal swabs or tracheal aspirates as infants cannot produce sputum) show normal upper respiratory flora. Stool shows mixed fecal flora (predominantly Bifidobacteria in breastfed infants; mixed enteric flora in formula-fed). White blood cells: Few or none in sterile specimens.
results.female_result: No organisms seen in sterile body fluids (blood, CSF, pleural fluid, urine obtained via catheter or suprapubic aspiration). For non-sterile sites: Normal commensal flora consistent with specimen site. For vaginal/genital specimens: In neonates (first few weeks of life), flora may resemble adults (Lactobacillus positive) due to maternal estrogen transfer. In older infants, the vaginal flora changes to a prepubertal pattern: mixed skin and enteric flora (diphtheroids, coagulase-negative staphylococci, coliforms) with scant or absent Lactobacillus. White blood cells: Few or none in sterile specimens.

results.male_result: Bowel gas pattern typically shows a non-specific, mosaic distribution throughout the abdomen, which is normal for infants due to aerophagia. Gas should be visible from the stomach to the rectum (assuming the infant is >24 hours old). Differentiation between small and large bowel is often indistinct due to the lack of developed haustra and valvulae conniventes. No evidence of bowel dilation, pneumatosis intestinalis, or portal venous gas. No free intraperitoneal air. Solid organ shadows are within normal limits, though the liver may occupy a relatively larger proportion of the abdomen compared to adults. Skeletal structures display normal immature ossification appropriate for age.

No evidence of bowel dilation, pneumatosis intestinalis, or portal venous gas. No free intraperitoneal air. Solid organ shadows are within normal limits, though the liver may occupy a relatively larger proportion of the abdomen compared to adults. Skeletal structures display normal immature ossification appropriate for age.

results.male_result: The colon demonstrates normal caliber and course appropriate for the infant's age and size. The cecum is correctly positioned in the right lower quadrant. The rectosigmoid ratio is normal, with the rectum caliber appearing wider than the sigmoid colon. Haustral markings are present but may appear shallower or less prominent compared to older children and adults. The sigmoid colon may appear redundant, which is a common variant in infants. No evidence of a transition zone, microcolon, strictures, or filling defects. No signs of intussusception. The mucosal outline is smooth.

results.male_result: Not applicable. Bitewing radiographs are typically not performed on infants as the primary posterior teeth (molars) have not yet erupted, and interproximal contacts have not been established. If dental imaging is required for trauma or pathology, periapical or occlusal views would show developing tooth buds within the alveolar bone rather than erupted posterior dentition.

results.male_result: Left hand and wrist radiograph demonstrates skeletal maturity consistent with chronological age. At birth, the carpal bones are cartilaginous and radiolucent (invisible on X-ray). The ossification centers for the Capitate and Hamate bones typically appear between 3 to 6 months of age. The distal radial epiphysis is usually not ossified during the first year. No evidence of rickets (metaphyseal cupping or fraying).
results.female_result: Left hand and wrist radiograph demonstrates skeletal maturity consistent with chronological age. At birth, the carpal bones are cartilaginous and radiolucent. The ossification centers for the Capitate and Hamate bones typically appear between 2 to 6 months of age (generally slightly earlier than males). The distal radial epiphysis is usually not ossified but may appear towards the end of the first year (around 10-12 months). No evidence of rickets.

results.male_result: Lungs: Bilateral lung fields are clear with no focal consolidation or masses. Bronchovascular markings are normal. Heart: Cardiac silhouette appears relatively globular and large due to the horizontal position of the heart and AP projection; a cardiothoracic ratio (CTR) of up to 0.60 (60%) is considered normal. Mediastinum: The thymus gland is prominent and widely visible (characteristic 'sail sign' or 'wave sign'), often widening the superior mediastinum, which is a normal physiological finding. Trachea is midline. Hila: Normal. Pleura: No effusion. Diaphragm: Smooth contours. Bony thorax: Ribs are horizontally oriented; visualized osseous structures are intact.

results.male_result: The bladder is positioned higher, largely within the abdomen rather than the deep pelvis. The contour is smooth and regular, with no filling defects, diverticula, or trabeculation. Bladder wall thickness is thin and uniform. Bladder capacity is age-appropriate (significantly smaller than adults, typically ranging from 30 to 100 mL depending on weight and specific age). No vesicoureteral reflux is demonstrated. During the voiding phase, the urethra appears patent with normal caliber; critically, there is no evidence of posterior urethral valves or strictures. Post-void residual is minimal.
results.female_result: The bladder is positioned higher, largely within the abdomen rather than the deep pelvis. The contour is smooth and regular, with no filling defects or diverticula. Bladder wall thickness is thin and uniform. Bladder capacity is age-appropriate (significantly smaller than adults, typically ranging from 30 to 100 mL depending on weight and specific age). No vesicoureteral reflux is demonstrated. The urethra appears normal in caliber and course without obstruction or evidence of ureteroceles. Post-void residual is minimal.

results.male_result: Patent lacrimal drainage system with contrast medium flowing freely from the puncta through the canaliculi into the lacrimal sac, then through the nasolacrimal duct into the inferior meatus of the nasal cavity. No filling defects, strictures, or obstruction observed. The lacrimal sac and nasolacrimal duct demonstrate normal size, caliber, and contour proportionate to the infant's age and developmental stage. Complete drainage of contrast into the nasal cavity is observed.

results.male_result: Standard functional defecography is generally not performed on infants due to the inability to cooperate with commands (squeeze, strain) and maintain a sitting posture. If contrast imaging of the rectum is performed (e.g., Barium Enema), the anal canal is significantly shorter than adults (typically < 1.5 cm), and the rectum should appear patent without narrowing, transition zones (suggestive of Hirschsprung's disease), or fistulas.
results.female_result: Standard functional defecography is generally not performed on infants due to the inability to cooperate with commands (squeeze, strain) and maintain a sitting posture. If contrast imaging is performed, the anal canal is significantly shorter than adults (typically < 1.5 cm). The rectum should be patent; typical adult measurements for rectocele or pelvic floor descent are not applicable. Anatomy should be checked for congenital fistulas or malformations.

results.male_result: Radiographic findings show age-appropriate dental development stages. Deciduous (primary) tooth germs are visible within the alveolar bone in various stages of calcification. By 6-12 months, erupted deciduous central or lateral incisors may be present with developing roots. Early mineralization of permanent first molar buds and permanent incisor tips may be visible deep within the jaw. The alveolar bone demonstrates normal developing trabecular pattern. No evidence of congenital anomalies (such as anodontia or supernumerary teeth), cysts, fractures, or pathological lesions. Dental follicles surrounding developing teeth are normal.
results.female_result: Radiographic findings show age-appropriate dental development stages. Deciduous (primary) tooth germs are visible within the alveolar bone in various stages of calcification. Early mineralization of permanent first molar buds and permanent incisor tips may be visible deep within the jaw. The alveolar bone demonstrates normal developing trabecular pattern. No evidence of congenital anomalies (such as anodontia or supernumerary teeth), cysts, fractures, or pathological lesions. Dental follicles surrounding developing teeth are normal.

The vertebral bodies should show appropriate ossification centers for the developmental stage, with homogeneous trabecular density and no signs of rachitic changes or compression.

Peak systolic velocities (PSV) are variable based on state (calm vs. crying) and heart rate, typically ranging from 60 to 120 cm/s in the common carotid artery. Spectral waveforms demonstrate a low-resistance pattern with continuous forward diastolic flow in the internal carotid artery. No evidence of congenital anomalies, dissection, or arteriovenous malformation.

results.male_result: Liver: Homogeneous enhancement pattern. Kidneys: Symmetric cortical enhancement with rapid arterial phase uptake and distinct corticomedullary differentiation; no focal masses. Spleen: Homogeneous enhancement. Major vessels: Patent. Contrast kinetics reflect faster wash-in and wash-out consistent with infant physiology, with no evidence of abnormal focal enhancement or malignant patterns.

No focal enlargement, nerve root avulsion, or discontinuity is observed. Normal echotexture without evidence of swelling or neuroma formation.

results.male_result: Globe shape is spherical and symmetric. Vitreous cavity is anechoic (clear); normal hyaloid remnants may occasionally be faintly visible in very young infants but significant opacities are absent. Retina is attached and flat against the choroid. Optic nerve appears normal but with smaller dimensions than adults. No intraocular masses, calcifications (indicative of retinoblastoma), or foreign bodies identified. Orbital fat shows normal homogeneous echogenicity.

In post-neonatal infants, the uterus regresses to a prepubertal tubular shape, where the cervix is often wider than or equal to the fundus (cervix-to-fundus ratio approx 2:1 or 1:1), length approx. 2.5-3.0 cm, with a thin linear endometrium. Ovaries: Small volumes (usually <1 cc). In neonates, small physiological cysts may be present due to maternal hormones; in older infants, ovaries are small and generally homogeneous. No adnexal masses. No significant free fluid.

Normal diaphragmatic excursion with respiration. No loculated fluid collections.

For healthy term infants, tibial SOS values typically range from 1480 to 1580 m/s, strictly correlating with gestational age and birth weight.

results.male_result: Both kidneys are normal in position and shape. Fetal lobulation may be present (normal variant). Normal systolic function: LVEF 56-78%, Fractional Shortening (FS) 28-44%. Anatomy: Situs solitus, levocardia, normal atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial connections. No congenital structural defects (e.g., VSD, large ASD, PDA). Valvular assessment: Valves thin and pliable with no stenosis or significant regurgitation. Normal coronary artery origins. No pericardial effusion.

results.female_result: This examination method (Transvaginal) is contraindicated in infants. Normal findings via Transabdominal Ultrasound are: Uterus: In neonates (0-3 months), the uterus is prominent due to maternal hormone influence, approx. 3-4 cm in length, tubular or spade-shaped, with a cervix-to-fundus ratio of 1:1 or 2:1 (cervix larger than fundus), and a visible endometrial line. In older infants (>3 months), the uterus regresses to a prepubertal state: smaller (2.5-3 cm), tubular, with an indistinct endometrium. Ovaries: Small, elongated, volume typically <1 cm³ (often <0.5 cm³), located high in the pelvis or lower abdomen; small follicles may be visible in newborns.

results.male_result: Liver: Homogeneous parenchymal echotexture, smooth contour, size appropriate for age (longitudinal span approx. 5-8 cm), no focal lesions. Gallbladder: Thin wall (<3 mm), anechoic lumen. Spleen: Homogeneous, length <6 cm. Pancreas: Normal size for age, often isoechoic to liver. Bladder: Smooth wall, anechoic urine. Prostate: Pre-pubertal, extremely small and indistinct, rarely visualized. Scrotum: Testes in scrotum, homogeneous echotexture, size approx. 1 cm long. Pylorus (if visualized): Muscle thickness <3 mm, channel length <15 mm. Head (if fontanelle open): Normal ventricular system, no intraventricular hemorrhage or periventricular leukomalacia.

results.male_result: Cornea: Central corneal thickness is variable; it may be thicker at birth due to physiological hydration (>550 μm) but rapidly deswells, typically averaging 520-580 μm; corneal diameter is smaller (<11 mm). Anterior chamber angle: Open angle configuration, though the angle recess and scleral spur may be less distinct due to immature development. Iris: Generally thinner than adults, smooth surfaces. Ciliary body: Immature development, appearing flatter and thinner with a shorter pars plana compared to the distinct triangular adult shape. Lens: The lens is more spherical (greater anteroposterior thickness relative to eye size) compared to adults. Sclera: Uniform echogenicity, though more pliable.

results.male_result: Carotid arteries: Bilateral common, internal, and external carotid arteries display normal anatomical course and patency. Vessel caliber is significantly smaller than adults. Intima-media thickness is negligible and smooth (typically < 0.4 mm). No atherosclerotic plaque. Flow velocities are variable based on age/cardiac output but generally demonstrate laminar flow. Abdominal aorta: Normal caliber proportionate to body surface area (diameter ranges approx. 0.6 cm to 1.2 cm depending on age in months), tapering distally. No evidence of coarctation, interruption, or aneurysmal dilatation. Extremity vessels: Arteries are patent with appropriate high-resistance waveforms in resting extremities. Veins (deep and superficial) are fully compressible, patent, with spontaneous flow; no evidence of catheter-associated thrombus or congenital vascular malformations.

results.male_result: Symmetric, intense radiotracer uptake is observed at the epiphyseal-metaphyseal growth complexes (growth plates) of the long bones, particularly prominent at the knees (distal femur, proximal tibia), ankles, wrists, and shoulders. This intense uptake is physiologic and indicative of active bone growth. The diaphyses of long bones show uniform, lower-intensity uptake. The calvarium demonstrates symmetric activity, potentially with increased uptake along the cranial sutures. The costochondral junctions may show prominent, symmetric uptake. The vertebral column and pelvis show symmetric tracer distribution. Kidneys are visualized bilaterally with normal excretion. Bladder activity is present. No focal asymmetric areas of abnormal uptake suggestive of osteomyelitis, fracture, or neoplasm are seen.

Normal sympathetic innervation.

results.male_result: Normal liquid gastric emptying study using Tc-99m labeled formula or breast milk: The radiotracer demonstrates progressive emptying from the stomach into the small bowel, typically following an exponential pattern (faster than solids). Half-emptying time (T1/2) is typically between 45 and 75 minutes, depending on formula composition. Gastric retention at 1 hour is typically <50-60%. No evidence of significant gastroesophageal reflux (GER) to the level of the upper esophagus or pulmonary aspiration. Visualization of the pylorus indicates no anatomical obstruction.

results.male_result: Prompt hepatic uptake of radiotracer (Tc-99m IDA derivative). The gallbladder is typically visualized. The critical normal finding is the visualization of radiotracer activity in the small bowel (duodenum/jejunum), which confirms the patency of the extrahepatic biliary ducts. While bowel excretion often occurs within 60 minutes, visualization within 4 to 24 hours on delayed images is still considered a result indicating biliary patency, effectively ruling out biliary atresia.

V/Q match: Concordance between ventilation and perfusion images with no mismatched defects. No evidence of right-to-left shunt (kidney or brain uptake).

results.male_result: Symmetric visualization of lymphatic channels with prompt radiotracer uptake. rapid transport of tracer from the injection site to regional lymph nodes (axillary or inguinal) due to shorter limb length and smaller body size. No evidence of lymphatic obstruction, dermal backflow (which would suggest lymphatic hypertension or valve failure), lymphatic aplasia/hypoplasia, or collateral pathways.

Prominent urinary activity is expected in the bladder and diaper area.

results.male_result: Examination is not clinically indicated for infants. If performed, there should be no significant cortical Pittsburgh Compound B (PIB) retention. Findings would be consistent with amyloid-negative status.

Brown adipose tissue (brown fat) uptake may be present in the neck, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and paraspinal regions depending on thermal regulation.

Liver and spleen demonstrate homogeneous background uptake. Kidneys and urinary bladder show expected excretory activity. Testes demonstrate negligible physiologic uptake. No abnormal hypermetabolic foci suggesting malignancy.

If clinically indicated (using I-123), newborns exhibit physiologically elevated uptake (can exceed 50-70% in the first 48 hours) due to the postnatal TSH surge. Values typically decline to adult-like ranges (10-35% at 24 hours) after the first few weeks of life.

Unlike adults, active hematopoietic (red) marrow is present in the entire axial skeleton (including prominent uptake in the skull) and the full length of the appendicular skeleton. Uptake extends to the distal parts of the long bones (humeri, radii, ulnae, femora, tibiae, and fibulae). Physiological uptake is seen in the liver and spleen.

results.male_result: Both kidneys demonstrate normal position and relative size; fetal lobulation is a common normal variant. Split renal function shows balanced differential function (each kidney 45-55% of total). Physiological renal immaturity is expected: GFR and ERPF are significantly lower than adult values. At birth, GFR is approximately 30-40 mL/min/1.73m², doubling by 2 months, and reaching 80-100 mL/min/1.73m² by 1 year. The time-activity curve may demonstrate slower uptake and excretion compared to adults due to immature tubular function and lower renal blood flow. Tc-99m MAG3 is the preferred radiotracer for this age group as it provides better image quality and functional data than DTPA in immature kidneys.

results.male_result: In infants, normal ICGA findings follow the same qualitative sequence as adults but with significantly faster hemodynamics. 1. Early Phase: Rapid sequential filling of choroidal arteries and choriocapillaris. The arm-to-choroid circulation time is much shorter than in adults (often <10 seconds) due to the shorter distance from the heart and faster heart rate. Large choroidal vessels appear hyperfluorescent. 2. Mid Phase: Vessel margins become less distinct rapidly as dye diffuses, causing diffuse hyperfluorescence of the choroidal tissue. 3. Late Phase: The 'inversion phenomenon' is observed (hypofluorescent large vessels against a hyperfluorescent stromal background), though the transition to this phase may occur sooner than the adult 15-minute marker due to faster metabolic clearance and circulation.

Normal sinus rhythm with age-appropriate heart rate (typically 100-160 bpm). Corrected Sinus Node Recovery Time (CSNRT) is significantly shorter than adults, typically < 275 ms. No evidence of accessory pathways and no inducible sustained arrhythmias.

results.male_result: Normal sinus rhythm throughout monitoring period; Heart rate typically 100-160 bpm while awake, with normal variation during sleep (may drop to 80-90 bpm); Pronounced respiratory sinus arrhythmia is a normal variant; No significant pauses (typically >2.0 seconds is considered significant for this age); No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia; No supraventricular tachycardia (infant SVT rates typically exceed 220 bpm); Rare premature atrial contractions (PACs); Rare premature ventricular contractions (PVCs); No significant ST-segment deviation; No atrioventricular block.

results.male_result: Background rhythm is age-dependent and rapidly maturing. 0-2 months: Low voltage irregular mixed frequencies. 3-4 months: A rhythmic posterior dominant rhythm (PDR) becomes identifiable at 3-4 Hz. 5-12 months: PDR frequency increases to 5-7 Hz (Theta range). The rhythm should react (attenuate) to eye opening by 3-6 months. Sleep architecture: Sleep spindles (12-14 Hz) appear around 2-3 months (initially asynchronous) and vertex sharp waves appear by 5 months. No focal slowing, persistent asymmetries, or epileptiform discharges.

results.male_result: Dominant frequency: Generally 2.5-3.7 cycles per minute (cpm) in full-term infants (similar to adults), though variability may be higher in neonates; Normal slow wave percentage: Increases with age, approaching adult levels (>70%) by 3-6 months; Power ratio: >1.0 (indicates response to milk/formula feeding).

results.male_result: EMG: No spontaneous activity at rest (no fibrillation potentials or positive sharp waves). Motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) generally show shorter duration (3-8 ms) and lower amplitude compared to adults due to smaller muscle fiber size. Recruitment pattern is assessed during spontaneous movement or crying (full interference pattern). NCS: Nerve conduction velocities (NCV) are significantly slower than adults due to incomplete myelination. At birth/early infancy, NCV is approximately 50% of adult values (20-30 m/s). By age 1, NCV increases to approximately 75% of adult values (35-50 m/s). Distal latencies may appear short due to small limb length but reflect slower conduction. CMAP and SNAP amplitudes are generally lower than adult values.

results.male_result: Testing is technically challenging due to inability to calibrate and lack of cooperation. Oculomotor function is developmentally immature compared to adults. Saccades: Longer latencies (often >250-300 ms) and hypometric. Smooth pursuit: Often saccadic (cog-wheel) or absent in early infancy; gain is significantly lower than adults (gradually improves after 3-4 months). Optokinetic nystagmus (OKN): Monocular asymmetry (Temporal-to-Nasal response > Nasal-to-Temporal) is considered normal in infants up to 3-5 months. Caloric/Vestibular: Vestibular response (nystagmus) is present at birth, but quantitative parameters (UW%, DP%) are difficult to calculate reliably due to calibration issues. Rotational responses are generally more robust and easier to elicit than caloric responses in this age group.

results.male_result: In infants (0-1 year), ERG responses are physiologically immature and rapidly developing. At birth, amplitudes are significantly lower than adults (typically 30-50% of adult values) and implicit times are prolonged (delayed latency). By 6-12 months, responses mature significantly. Scotopic Rod b-wave amplitude: typically 100-300 μV (increasing with age). Combined response a-wave and b-wave are present but lower amplitude. Photopic 30 Hz flicker amplitude: typically 40-100 μV in early infancy, increasing towards adult values by age 1. Implicit times are longer than adults but shorten rapidly during the first year.

results.male_result: Short-term (5-min): SDNN 20-50 ms, RMSSD 15-45 ms. Long-term (24-hour): SDNN 50-100 ms. Values are lower than adults due to higher baseline heart rates.

results.male_result: Absolute latencies and conduction velocities are highly variable and age-dependent. In infants, peripheral conduction velocity is approximately 50% of adult values at birth. While limb length is shorter (which would shorten latency), the slower conduction due to incomplete myelination often results in absolute latencies that are similar to or slightly longer than adults (e.g., Median N20 may range 18-25 ms; Tibial P37 may range 35-50 ms in neonates). Central Conduction Time (CCT) is significantly prolonged.

results.male_result: Latency: Palm approximately 0.6-1.1 seconds, Sole approximately 1.0-1.5 seconds (values correlate positively with body length/height). Amplitude: Highly variable, typically 0.1-2.0 mV. Waveform: Often monophasic or biphasic. Response is present in healthy full-term infants.

results.male_result: Visual Evoked Potential (VEP): Flash VEP is typically performed as infants cannot fixate for Pattern VEP. The major positive component (often P2 in flash VEP) has a prolonged latency at birth (approx. 150-170 ms) compared to adult P100. Latency decreases rapidly, reaching near-adult levels (100-120 ms) by 6-12 months. Electroretinogram (ERG): Both scotopic and photopic responses are present at birth but with significantly lower amplitudes (approx. 25-50% of adult levels) and longer implicit times. Values increase rapidly during the first year, often reaching adult ranges by 6-12 months. EOG/PERG: Generally not performed or difficult to interpret due to lack of fixation and cooperation.

If Pattern-Reversal VEP (PR-VEP) is obtainable: P100 latency is prolonged, ranging from 160-180 ms at birth and decreasing to near-adult values (100-110 ms) by 12 months.

results.male_result: Not applicable - Abscess aspiration is a diagnostic/therapeutic procedure performed only when an abscess is present. In healthy individuals, no abscess exists, therefore this examination is not performed. Normal finding: No abscess present, no aspiration indicated.

results.male_result: Normal bone architecture with signs of active remodeling and skeletal growth. Bone marrow cellularity is significantly higher than in adults, typically ranging from 80% to 100% (predominantly red marrow). Normal trilineage hematopoiesis is present. The myeloid to erythroid (M:E) ratio is typically between 2:1 and 4:1, though it may be lower in the immediate neonatal period. A markedly increased population of hematogones (benign B-cell precursors) is a normal physiological finding. Osteoblasts and osteoclasts are prominent due to growth. No fibrosis, necrosis, or malignant cells.

results.male_result: Cellularity: 80-100% (significantly hypercellular compared to adults). Myeloid to erythroid (M:E) ratio: Variable; often 2:1 to 4:1, but can be elevated in the first days of life, then stabilize. Lymphocytes: Increased numbers (physiological lymphocytosis), often 30-50% of nucleated cells. Hematogones: Frequently present (benign B-cell precursors) and may be numerous. Megakaryocytes: Adequate. Trilineage hematopoiesis present. Iron stores: Present (variable; high at birth, may deplete by 4-6 months). Blasts: <5% (morphologically).

results.male_result: Normal brain parenchyma with age-appropriate cortical lamination and white matter architecture. Myelination is active and ongoing; the extent of myelination must be consistent with the infant's chronological age (naturally less extensive than adults). Neurons display normal morphology and migration patterns, with no evidence of cortical dysplasia or heterotopias. Cellular density may be physiologically higher than in adults. No evidence of inflammation, vasculitis, viral inclusions, metabolic storage deposits, gliosis, or neoplastic cells.

A small number of neutrophils may be present in healthy neonates without indicating infection. No blast cells or tumor cells present.

results.male_result: Glomerular Basement Membrane (GBM) harmonic mean thickness is physiologically significantly thinner than in adults, typically ranging from 150 to 250 nm. The GBM displays a continuous trilaminar structure, though the lamina densa may appear less prominent than in adults. Podocytes show intact foot processes with normal interdigitations; diffuse foot process effacement is absent (critical for excluding congenital nephrotic syndrome). Endothelial cells exhibit patent fenestrations. The mesangial matrix is within normal limits for age. No electron-dense immune deposits, amyloid fibrils, or organized substructures are identified in the subepithelial, subendothelial, or mesangial spaces. Intracellular organelles maintain normal ultrastructural morphology.

In infants after the neonatal period, the smear is atrophic (pre-pubertal pattern), consisting predominantly of parabasal and small intermediate cells. The smear should be negative for malignant cells and specific infectious agents.

results.male_result: Developing microbial community characterized by lower alpha diversity (e.g., lower Shannon index) compared to adults. Composition is highly dynamic and varies by age (months). In healthy breastfed infants, the gut is typically dominated by Actinobacteria (specifically the genus Bifidobacterium), often exceeding 50-70% relative abundance. Proteobacteria (e.g., Enterobacteriaceae) are naturally more abundant than in adults as facultative anaerobes consume oxygen. Firmicutes (Lactobacillus, Streptococcus) are present, while Bacteroidetes generally appear in lower proportions until the introduction of solid foods. Formula-fed infants may show higher alpha diversity earlier with relatively lower Bifidobacterium and higher levels of Firmicutes and Bacteroides.

results.male_result: Negative response (non-responder): Failure to meet pediatric responder criteria. Historically, this is defined as failure to achieve a decrease in mean pulmonary arterial pressure (mPAP) of ≥ 20%, with unchanged or increased cardiac index, and unchanged or decreased pulmonary-to-systemic vascular resistance ratio (Barst criteria). While recent guidelines align closer to adult criteria (drop in mPAP ≥ 10 mmHg to absolute value ≤ 40 mmHg), the absolute cutoff of 40 mmHg must be interpreted relative to the infant's lower systemic blood pressure. In healthy infants past the neonatal transition (1-3 months), pulmonary hemodynamics resemble adults, and the test is not indicated.

While recent guidelines align closer to adult criteria (drop in mPAP ≥ 10 mmHg to absolute value ≤ 40 mmHg), the absolute cutoff of 40 mmHg must be interpreted relative to the infant's lower systemic blood pressure. In healthy infants past the neonatal transition (1-3 months), pulmonary hemodynamics resemble adults, and the test is not indicated.

results.male_result: Standard adult provocation tests (Valsalva maneuver, deep breathing test, active standing 30:15 ratio) are not applicable due to the infant's inability to follow instructions and stand. Assessment relies primarily on passive monitoring (Holter) and physical examination. Resting Heart Rate: 100-160 bpm (varies by age). Heart Rate Variability (HRV) parameters (SDNN, RMSSD) are generally lower than adults due to higher baseline heart rates and immature autonomic regulation (e.g., SDNN often 20-60 ms). Sympathetic Skin Response (SSR): Latencies are shorter than adults due to smaller body size.

results.male_result: Infant contrast sensitivity is immature and significantly lower than adult levels, generally 1.0-2.0 log units lower depending on the spatial frequency. The Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF) curve is shifted to lower spatial frequencies (left shift). At birth to 3 months, peak sensitivity is at very low frequencies (often < 1 cycle per degree). By 6-12 months, the curve shape matures and peak sensitivity shifts towards higher frequencies (approx. 2-4 cpd), but overall sensitivity remains below adult thresholds. Results are evaluated based on behavioral fixation and following of low-contrast stimuli.

results.male_result: GH Stimulation Test (Glucagon/Arginine preferred): Peak GH >7-10 ng/mL (Neonates/infants have higher basal GH; Insulin Tolerance Test is contraindicated); ACTH Stimulation Test: Peak cortisol >18-20 mcg/dL (500-550 nmol/L), though preterm infants may show lower response; GnRH Stimulation Test: Age-dependent response. During 'minipuberty' (approx. 2 weeks to 6 months), LH response is robust and often exceeds FSH (LH peak >5-10 IU/L). After 6 months, the HPA axis becomes quiescent (prepubertal nadir) with low/blunted LH response; Water Deprivation Test: Maximal urine osmolality reaches ~600-700 mOsm/kg (renal concentrating ability is immature compared to adults).
results.female_result: GH Stimulation Test (Glucagon/Arginine preferred): Peak GH >7-10 ng/mL (Neonates/infants have higher basal GH; Insulin Tolerance Test is contraindicated); ACTH Stimulation Test: Peak cortisol >18-20 mcg/dL (500-550 nmol/L); GnRH Stimulation Test: Age-dependent response. During 'minipuberty' (approx. 2 weeks to 2-3 years), FSH response is dominant and exaggerated (FSH >> LH), differing from the male pattern. After minipuberty, response is blunted (prepubertal); Water Deprivation Test: Maximal urine osmolality reaches ~600-700 mOsm/kg (renal concentrating ability is immature compared to adults).

Voluntary equilibration maneuvers (Toynbee or Valsalva) cannot be performed; function is assessed passively by evaluating middle ear aeration.

results.male_result: For most infants (0-9 months), gait analysis is not applicable as independent walking has not yet developed. For older infants (10-12 months) starting to take independent steps: The gait is characterized by a wide base of support to maintain balance. Arms are typically held in a 'high guard' position (abducted and flexed at shoulders) with no reciprocal arm swing. Foot contact is flat-footed (no heel strike). Steps are short, rapid, and often uneven (staccato gait). There is increased hip and knee flexion during the swing phase. Frequent loss of balance is normal.
results.female_result: For most infants (0-9 months), gait analysis is not applicable as independent walking has not yet developed. For older infants (10-12 months) starting to take independent steps: The gait is characterized by a wide base of support to maintain balance. Arms are typically held in a 'high guard' position (abducted and flexed at shoulders) with no reciprocal arm swing. Foot contact is flat-footed (no heel strike). Steps are short, rapid, and often uneven (staccato gait). There is increased hip and knee flexion during the swing phase. Frequent loss of balance is normal.

results.male_result: During the 'minipuberty' phase (typically 0-6 months): The HPG axis is transiently active. Basal LH and FSH are detectable. Post-stimulation Peak LH is often > 10 mIU/mL, and typically Peak LH > Peak FSH. After 6-9 months: The axis becomes quiescent (prepubertal); Basal LH < 0.3 mIU/mL and Peak LH usually < 5-8 mIU/mL.
results.female_result: During the 'minipuberty' phase (typically 0-12+ months): The HPG axis is active with FSH dominance. Peak FSH is significantly elevated (often > 20 mIU/mL) and typically Peak FSH > Peak LH. Peak LH is generally lower than in males of the same age. Towards the end of infancy, responses diminish as the axis enters the childhood quiescent phase.

results.male_result: GnRH stimulation: During 'mini-puberty' (1-6 months), a robust response is normal with LH peak often >10 mIU/mL (LH dominant over FSH). After 6 months, response blunts to prepubertal levels (LH peak <5-8 mIU/mL). ACTH stimulation: Cortisol rises to >18-20 μg/dL (500-550 nmol/L). GH stimulation (Glucagon/Arginine): Growth hormone peaks >7-10 ng/mL (thresholds vary by assay).
results.female_result: GnRH stimulation: During 'mini-puberty' (0-24 months), a robust response is normal with FSH peak often >10-20 mIU/mL (FSH dominant over LH). After 2 years, response blunts to prepubertal levels. ACTH stimulation: Cortisol rises to >18-20 μg/dL (500-550 nmol/L). GH stimulation (Glucagon/Arginine): Growth hormone peaks >7-10 ng/mL.

results.male_result: In neonates and early infants, hepatic clearance is physiologically slower. Plasma Disappearance Rate (PDR) is typically lower than adults, ranging approximately 12-18% per minute. Consequently, the retention rate at 15 minutes (R15) may be higher, often between 10-20%, without indicating pathology. By late infancy (approaching 1 year), values begin to approximate adult norms (PDR 18-25%, R15 < 10%).

results.male_result: Schirmer Test I: Unreliable in awake infants due to reflex tearing; under anesthesia or if successful, values are typically ≥10 mm/5 min (basal secretion is low in newborns <2-4 weeks). Phenol Red Thread Test: Average 15-24 mm in 15 seconds. Tear Break-Up Time (TBUT): Typically stable, ≥10 seconds. Dye Disappearance Test (DDT): Fluorescein dye disappears from the tear meniscus within 5 minutes (preferred over Jones Test for this age).

results.male_result: Standard exercise-based metabolic loading (cardiopulmonary exercise testing, graded exercise) is not applicable. For diagnostic metabolic nutrient challenges (e.g., Oral Glucose Tolerance Test): Blood glucose returns to normal ranges within 2-3 hours post-load; No pathological hypoglycemia (glucose maintained >50-60 mg/dL or >2.8-3.3 mmol/L depending on specific age in months); Lactate remains within infant reference limits (typically <2.5 mmol/L); No development of severe metabolic acidosis or hyperammonemia during the challenge.

results.male_result: Test is generally not performed on infants due to technical limitations and safety risks. If performed in specialized settings, a negative response is defined strictly by the absence of objective clinical signs: no paroxysmal sneezing, no anterior rhinorrhea, no nasal rubbing, and no signs of respiratory distress. Objective measures like Acoustic Rhinometry (passive) should show no significant reduction in nasal volume, but standard adult metrics requiring cooperation (PNIF, VAS scores) are not applicable.

results.male_result: Negative response: No significant change in muscle tone, sucking reflex, or crying strength following the administration of neostigmine. In a healthy infant, baseline muscle tone remains normal. The test is considered negative when there is no objective improvement in hypotonia, feeding difficulties, or respiratory effort, as these symptoms are absent in healthy subjects. Dosage is strictly weight-based (typically 0.04 mg/kg IM or 0.02 mg/kg IV).
