Dosage is strictly weight-based (typically 0.04 mg/kg IM or 0.02 mg/kg IV).

results.male_result: Neurodevelopmental milestones appropriate for chronological age (or corrected age). Gross Motor: Progression includes head control, rolling over, sitting without support, crawling, pulling to stand, and potentially cruising/walking. Fine Motor: Progression includes reflexive grasp, voluntary reaching, transferring objects between hands, and pincer grasp. Cognitive/Adaptive: Demonstrates visual tracking, awareness of environment, object permanence, and cause-and-effect exploration. Communication: Progression from cooing to babbling, responding to name, and understanding simple gestures. Social-Emotional: Presence of social smile, eye contact, stranger anxiety (appropriate for stage), and attachment behaviors. Scores on standardized developmental scales (e.g., Bayley Scales, Gesell) within normal limits.

Cognitive/Adaptive: Demonstrates visual tracking, awareness of environment, object permanence, and cause-and-effect exploration. results.male_result: Demonstrates ability to fixate on and follow a moving target (such as a light or face) with conjugate eye movements. Full range of motion is evident in all directions (may be assessed via vestibulo-ocular reflex/Doll's head maneuver in neonates). By 3-4 months, smooth pursuit is established and convergence is consistent. Corneal light reflexes are symmetric (normal Hirschberg test). No constant strabismus (tropia) observed. Transient, intermittent eye misalignment may be present in the first few weeks but should resolve by 4-6 months.
results.female_result: Demonstrates ability to fixate on and follow a moving target (such as a light or face) with conjugate eye movements. By 3-4 months, smooth pursuit is established and convergence is consistent. Corneal light reflexes are symmetric (normal Hirschberg test). No constant strabismus (tropia) observed. Transient, intermittent eye misalignment may be present in the first few weeks but should resolve by 4-6 months.

results.male_result: Normal olfactory function: The infant possesses a functional sense of smell at birth. Normal responses are behavioral reflexes to strong or novel odors (e.g., alcohol, peppermint, anise), including facial grimacing, head withdrawal (aversion), sneezing, changes in sucking frequency, or alterations in heart rate and respiratory rhythm. The infant demonstrates the ability to localize odors and shows a preference for the scent of the mother's breast milk.

The infant demonstrates the ability to localize odors and shows a preference for the scent of the mother's breast milk.

results.male_result: Axial length (AL): At birth ~16.5-17.5 mm, increasing rapidly to ~20.5-21.5 mm by 1 year; Anterior chamber depth (ACD): ~2.0-2.8 mm (deepens with age); Lens thickness (LT): ~3.5-4.0 mm; Corneal curvature (K): Steep at birth (~47.0-52.0 D), flattening to ~43.0-45.0 D by 1 year; White-to-white (WTW): ~9.8-11.0 mm; Central corneal thickness (CCT): ~520-580 μm.
results.female_result: Axial length (AL): At birth ~16.2-17.2 mm, increasing rapidly to ~20.0-21.0 mm by 1 year; Anterior chamber depth (ACD): ~1.9-2.7 mm (deepens with age); Lens thickness (LT): ~3.5-4.0 mm; Corneal curvature (K): Steep at birth (~47.5-52.5 D), flattening to ~43.5-45.5 D by 1 year; White-to-white (WTW): ~9.5-10.8 mm; Central corneal thickness (CCT): ~520-580 μm.

results.male_result: Visual acuity: Assessment relies on fixation behavior (Central, Steady, Maintained) and following objects, or preferential looking tests (e.g., Teller Acuity Cards). Acuity is developmentally low at birth (approx. 20/400 to 20/600), improving to roughly 20/50 to 20/100 by 12 months. Alignment: Small, intermittent exotropia is common and normal in the first few months. Eyes should be consistently aligned (orthotropic) by 4-6 months. Constant esotropia at any age or exotropia persisting beyond 6 months is abnormal. Ocular motility: Full range of motion should be present. Smooth pursuit movements typically develop by 6-8 weeks; earlier tracking may be saccadic. Stereopsis: Gross stereopsis begins to develop around 3-5 months.
results.female_result: Visual acuity: Assessment relies on fixation behavior (Central, Steady, Maintained) and following objects, or preferential looking tests (e.g., Teller Acuity Cards). Alignment: Small, intermittent exotropia is common and normal in the first few months. Eyes should be consistently aligned (orthotropic) by 4-6 months. Constant esotropia at any age or exotropia persisting beyond 6 months is abnormal. Ocular motility: Full range of motion should be present. Smooth pursuit movements typically develop by 6-8 weeks; earlier tracking may be saccadic. Stereopsis: Gross stereopsis begins to develop around 3-5 months.

results.male_result: Present and robust otoacoustic emissions bilaterally, typically classified as a 'Pass'. In infants, response amplitudes are typically larger (higher amplitude) than in adults due to smaller ear canal volume enhancing sound pressure. Transient evoked otoacoustic emissions (TEOAEs) demonstrate reproducibility ≥70% with signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) ≥6 dB, particularly in frequencies essential for speech development. Distortion product otoacoustic emissions (DPOAEs) show SNR ≥6 dB at tested frequencies. Indicates intact outer hair cell function.
results.female_result: Present and robust otoacoustic emissions bilaterally, typically classified as a 'Pass'. In infants, response amplitudes are typically larger (higher amplitude) than in adults due to smaller ear canal volume. Females generally exhibit slightly higher amplitude OAEs than males. Transient evoked otoacoustic emissions (TEOAEs) demonstrate reproducibility ≥70% with signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) ≥6 dB. Distortion product otoacoustic emissions (DPOAEs) show SNR ≥6 dB at tested frequencies. Indicates intact outer hair cell function.

results.male_result: Basal Acid Output (BAO): Typically 0.05–0.15 mEq/kg/h (absolute value usually < 2 mEq/h); Maximal/Peak Acid Output (PAO): Typically 0.30–0.60 mEq/kg/h. Values are strictly dependent on body weight.

results.male_result: Minimal Erythema Dose (MED) is physiologically lower than that of adults due to thinner stratum corneum and lower melanin content; no immediate wheal or flare reactions; no blistering or abnormal persistent erythema at doses adjusted for infant skin sensitivity.

results.male_result: Not applicable. Standard Computerized Dynamic Posturography (CDP) and Sensory Organization Tests (SOT) require independent, stable standing balance, which is not developmentally present in infants (0-12 months). Normal postural findings for this age group involve the acquisition of motor milestones: head control (3-4 months), independent sitting (6-8 months), and pulling to stand (9-12 months).

results.male_result: Mental status: Alert, wakeful, and responsive to environmental stimuli (e.g., visual tracking of faces, turning towards sounds). Appearance: Well-nourished, hygiene consistent with adequate care. Behavior: Exhibits appropriate attachment behaviors (e.g., social smiling, eye contact, reaching for caregiver). Soothable when crying. No pathological lethargy or excessive/high-pitched irritability. Speech/Vocalization: Cooing or babbling appropriate for developmental age; no language regression. Mood/Affect: Varies appropriately with physical needs (hunger, wet diaper) and social interaction; distinct periods of calmness and alertness. Cognition: Demonstrates age-appropriate sensorimotor development (e.g., grasping, object permanence depending on specific month age).

results.male_result: Standard voluntary spirometry is not applicable. Using infant-specific techniques (e.g., Raised Volume Rapid Thoracoabdominal Compression): FVC, FEV0.5 (Forced Expiratory Volume in 0.5 seconds), and flows (e.g., V'maxFRC) should be within normal limits (typically Z-scores between -1.64 and +1.64, or >80% of predicted) based on crown-heel length and ethnicity. Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) measured via plethysmography or gas dilution should be within normal limits for length. Tidal breathing analysis should show a normal tPTEF/tE ratio.

results.male_result: generally not applicable or unreliable. Standard sensibility tests (thermal and electrical) require subjective feedback and cognitive understanding which infants do not possess. Additionally, newly erupted primary teeth often yield false negative results due to immature neural development (lack of myelinated A-delta fibers) and open apices. Normal pulp health is assessed via clinical signs: normal tooth color, absence of swelling, fistula, or abnormal mobility.

results.male_result: Physiological hyperopia (farsightedness) is normal for infants. The mean refractive error is typically around +2.00 Diopters (D). A spherical equivalent range of +0.50 D to +3.50 D is generally considered within normal limits. Mild to moderate astigmatism (up to 2.00 D) is common in newborns and often decreases during the first year of life.

results.male_result: Total nasal airway resistance is significantly higher than adults due to smaller nasal anatomy, typically ranging from 0.50 to over 1.50 Pa/cm³/s (decreasing with age). Absolute nasal airflow is significantly lower than 500 cm³/s due to smaller lung volumes. Infants are obligate nasal breathers, so any asymmetry or obstruction is clinically significant.

results.male_result: Right atrial pressure (RAP): 2-7 mmHg (mean); Right ventricular pressure (RVP): 15-30/2-5 mmHg (systolic/diastolic); Pulmonary artery pressure (PAP): 15-30/5-15 mmHg (systolic/diastolic), mean 10-20 mmHg; Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP): 5-12 mmHg; Cardiac output (CO): Approximately 0.6-2.0 L/min (varies significantly with weight); Cardiac index (CI): 3.5-5.5 L/min/m²; Pulmonary vascular resistance index (PVRI): < 3 Wood Units·m²; Mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO2): 65-75%.

results.male_result: Salivary secretion volume is age-dependent. Neonates: Very low secretion (sufficient to maintain moist mucosa). 3-12 months: Secretion increases significantly; physiological drooling is normal due to immature swallowing coordination. Salivary amylase activity is low at birth (10-20% of adult levels) and increases gradually. pH: 6.0-7.5. Oral mucosa should be consistently moist.

results.male_result: Capillary morphology: Immature structure; often appears as a primitive plexus, simple dots, or commas rather than distinct hairpin loops, especially in newborns and early infancy (0-6 months). Gradual transition to loop formation occurs by 12 months. Capillary density: Variable due to developmental stage. Background visibility: High transparency; subpapillary venous plexus is often clearly visible due to thinner skin. Vasomotor response: High lability; physiological mottling (cutis marmorata) is common. Transcutaneous oxygen pressure (TcPO2): 50-80 mmHg.

results.male_result: Subjective reporting (THI, VAS) is not applicable. Normal finding constitutes the absence of objective tinnitus (e.g., vascular bruits, palatal myoclonus) detectable by the examiner upon auscultation. The infant displays normal auditory behavior and comfort without signs of unexplained irritability or ear-tugging that might suggest phantom sound perception.

results.male_result: For infants < 6 months (using 1000 Hz probe tone): Normal result is a single peaked tympanogram. Ear canal volume (ECV) 0.3-0.8 mL. For infants > 6 months (using 226 Hz probe tone): Type A tympanogram with peak compliance 0.2-0.9 mL, peak pressure -150 to +50 daPa. Acoustic reflex thresholds: 70-100 dB HL (often difficult to test due to movement).

results.male_result: Uroflowmetry: Maximum flow rate (Qmax) typically 5-15 mL/s depending on volume and age, voided volume varies by weight (approx. 30-100 mL), bell-shaped flow curve. Cystometry: Capacity estimated by formula [(age in years + 2) × 30 mL] or approx. 7 mL/kg in infants; bladder compliance should be age-appropriate (typically >10-15 mL/cmH2O for infants); no uninhibited detrusor contractions (though infantile bladder may show small spontaneous waves); stable detrusor during filling. Pressure-Flow Study: Detrusor pressure at maximum flow (PdetQmax) is often higher than adults, ranging 40-80 cmH2O. Post-void residual (PVR) volume <5-10 mL or <10% of capacity. Electromyography shows coordinated sphincter relaxation during voiding.

results.male_result: Evaluation primarily relies on objective physiological measures and behavioral observation rather than voluntary feedback. Rotational Chair: Robust vestibular-ocular reflex (VOR) response; VOR gain is typically higher than adults (often nearing 1.0); Phase leads are higher (shorter time constant) at low frequencies due to system immaturity. cVEMP: Present, but latencies are significantly shorter than adults due to smaller head size (P13 approx 8-12ms, N23 approx 16-20ms). vHIT: Normal VOR gain (~1.0), no corrective saccades. Oculomotor: Absence of spontaneous nystagmus; voluntary saccade and smooth pursuit testing is not feasible; optokinetic nystagmus may be elicited using a full-field drum. Postural tests (Romberg/Fukuda): Not applicable.

Oculomotor: Absence of spontaneous nystagmus; voluntary saccade and smooth pursuit testing is not feasible; optokinetic nystagmus may be elicited using a full-field drum. results.male_result: Visual development is rapid. Newborns: ~20/400 to 20/600 (perceive light/motion); 3 months: ~20/200; 6 months: ~20/60 to 20/100; 12 months: ~20/50. Functional assessment: Ability to fixate on and follow an object/face (CSM: Central, Steady, Maintained).
results.female_result: Same as male result.

results.male_result: Testing is performed using behavioral techniques (e.g., introducing a light or toy from the periphery). The infant should demonstrate a visual reflex or behavioral response (turning eyes or head) toward the stimulus. At birth, the visual field is physiologically constricted (limited to approximately 30 degrees). By 6 months, it extends to about 60-70 degrees horizontally. By 12 months, the field approaches adult limits. Responses should be symmetrical on both sides.

results.male_result: Visual acuity: Immature at birth (approx. 20/400 to 20/600), improving to approx. 20/50 to 20/60 by 12 months; assessed by ability to 'Fix and Follow' a face/object or Preferential Looking (e.g., Teller Acuity Cards). Color vision: Developing; basic red-green discrimination by 2 months, full trichromatic vision by 4-6 months. Stereopsis: Absent at birth, emerges around 3-5 months. Visual fields: Restricted at birth, expanding rapidly during the first year. Pupillary reflexes: Reactive to light (PERRL), though pupils may be smaller (miosis). Ocular alignment: Intermittent misalignment is normal up to 3-4 months; eyes should be aligned (orthotropic) by 6 months.

results.male_result: Urine osmolality increases to >600 mOsm/kg (infants have physiologically lower maximal concentrating ability than adults, typically peaking around 600-700 mOsm/kg); Urine specific gravity >1.015-1.020; Plasma osmolality remains <300 mOsm/kg; Test is terminated when urine concentration stabilizes, or body weight loss reaches 3-5% (strict limit).

results.male_result: Normal peritoneal fluid: Appearance - clear, straw-colored; Volume - very minimal (trace amounts present physiologically for lubrication, strictly proportional to body surface area); Specific gravity - < 1.016; Total protein - < 2.5 g/dL; Albumin - < 1.5 g/dL; Cell count - < 500 cells/μL; WBC - < 250 cells/μL with predominantly mononuclear cells; RBC - absent or minimal; Glucose - approximately equal to serum glucose; LDH - < 200 IU/L or less than serum LDH; Amylase - < 50 U/L; Culture - no bacterial growth; Cytology - negative for malignant cells

results.male_result: Positive response: Localized erythema (flare) and wheal formation at the injection site. A sweating response should be present, though the intensity and volume of sweat are often lower than in adults due to the functional immaturity of sweat glands in early infancy. The diameter of the erythema may vary and should be evaluated relative to the size of the infant's limb, rather than strictly adhering to adult dimensional standards.
results.female_result: Positive response: Localized erythema (flare) and wheal formation at the injection site. The diameter of the erythema may vary and should be evaluated relative to the size of the infant's limb, rather than strictly adhering to adult dimensional standards.

results.male_result: Successful cannulation confirmed by selectivity index (adrenal vein cortisol/peripheral vein cortisol) ≥2:1 (unstimulated) or ≥3:1 (ACTH-stimulated). Normal bilateral adrenal function shows lateralization index (aldosterone/cortisol ratio of dominant side divided by non-dominant side) <4:1, indicating no unilateral aldosterone hypersecretion. Interpretation is generally extrapolated from adult criteria.
results.female_result: Successful cannulation confirmed by selectivity index (adrenal vein cortisol/peripheral vein cortisol) ≥2:1 (unstimulated) or ≥3:1 (ACTH-stimulated). results.male_result: Synovial fluid: Clear to pale yellow, viscous. WBC <200 cells/μL, polymorphonuclear leukocytes <25%, no crystals, negative Gram stain and culture, glucose level approximates serum glucose.

results.male_result: Not applicable. This examination is intended for monitoring the female reproductive cycle.
results.female_result: Not applicable. Infants are prepubertal and do not experience ovulation or menstrual cycles. Consequently, the biphasic temperature pattern associated with the follicular and luteal phases does not exist.

results.male_result: Skin appears healthy, soft, and smooth with abundant subcutaneous fat and normal turgor appropriate for infancy. Skin folds are symmetric on the extremities. Color is consistent with ethnicity; no pathological lesions, significant jaundice, widespread rashes, or abnormal masses are visible. Benign physiologic variants (e.g., dermal melanocytosis or mild erythema) may be noted but are distinct from pathology. Anatomical structures, including the head and limbs, appear symmetric without dysmorphic features.

results.male_result: In healthy infants without pathological conditions, diagnostic nerve block is not routinely performed. When performed for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes: Expected response is assessed objectively rather than subjectively. Normal response includes a lack of motor response (withdrawal reflex) to noxious stimuli in the targeted nerve distribution, potential localized vasodilation (warming), and hemodynamic stability (no spike in heart rate or blood pressure) upon stimulation of the area. Signs of motor weakness or flaccidity in the specific muscle groups innervated by the targeted nerve are expected during the effective period.
results.female_result: In healthy infants without pathological conditions, diagnostic nerve block is not routinely performed. results.male_result: Not applicable for healthy screening. If the procedure is technically performed (typically under general anesthesia or heavy sedation), the physiological sign of a successful block is a rapid increase in skin temperature (typically >1.5°C to 3°C) in the affected extremity compared to the contralateral side, accompanied by visible vasodilation (flushing/erythema) and increased perfusion index on pulse oximetry. Subjective reporting is impossible; assessment relies entirely on objective hemodynamic and thermoregulatory changes.

results.female_result: Not applicable. This procedure is not performed on infants. Anatomically, the infant uterus is immature, very small (approximately 2.5 to 3.5 cm in total length), and the endometrium is thin and inactive (atrophic) due to the absence of estrogen stimulation. There is no menstrual cycle or reproductive function.

results.male_result: Due to anatomical size limitations, the distinct fractionation (A, B, C bile) and volumes defined for adults are not applicable to infants. Normal result indicates the successful aspiration of bile-stained fluid (golden yellow to green) upon stimulation, confirming biliary tract patency. The fluid should be clear, not turbid, with no evidence of blood or pus. Microscopically: <5 leukocytes/HPF, no parasites (e.g., Giardia lamblia), and no bacteria.

results.male_result: Not applicable - Exploratory laparotomy is a diagnostic/therapeutic surgical procedure performed only when pathology (e.g., necrotizing enterocolitis, intestinal malrotation, volvulus, congenital atresia) is suspected. results.male_result: Developmental milestones appropriate for chronological age (or corrected age if premature). Motor: Normal muscle tone, achievement of gross motor skills (head control, rolling, sitting, crawling, standing) and fine motor skills (grasping, reaching, pincer grasp). Cognitive: Visual tracking, object permanence, exploration of objects, and simple cause-and-effect understanding. Language: Pre-verbal skills including cooing, babbling, responding to name, and gesture use. Social-Emotional: Social smile, sustained eye contact, engagement with caregivers, and joint attention. Performance within the average range on standardized infant developmental scales (e.g., Bayley Scales).

results.male_result: General assessment: Active, vigorous cry, good muscle tone, reactive to parents and environment. ASA Physical Status Classification: ASA I (normal healthy infant) or ASA II (mild systemic disease). Airway assessment: Absence of craniofacial anomalies (e.g., micrognathia), no loose teeth (if erupted). Note: Mallampati is often inapplicable; assessment focuses on anatomical normality (large tongue and anterior larynx are normal for age). Cardiovascular: Heart rate within normal limit for age (typically 100-160 bpm), no pathological murmurs, good peripheral perfusion, no diaphoresis or fatigue during feeding. Pulmonary: Respiratory rate within normal limit for age (30-60 bpm), SpO2 ≥95% on room air, lungs clear. CRITICAL: No signs of current or recent (within 2-4 weeks) Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) such as rhinorrhea, cough, or fever. Renal/Hepatic: Normal hydration (adequate wet diapers). Nutritional status: Weight and length tracking along appropriate WHO growth chart percentiles. Birth History: Term gestation preferred; no history of unresolved apnea/bradycardia. NPO status confirmed (differs from adults). Informed consent obtained from parents/guardians.

results.item_name: Blood and Urine Organic Acid Analysis
results.male_result: No abnormal organic acid peaks detected. Normal metabolic intermediates present within age-specific reference ranges. Blood Lactic acid < 2.5 mmol/L (slightly higher upper limit than adults). Urine organic acid levels (normalized to creatinine) are significantly higher than adult values due to low muscle mass and creatinine excretion in infants. Mild dicarboxylic aciduria (adipic, suberic, sebacic acids) may be present and normal, particularly in formula-fed infants containing MCT oil. No specific pathological metabolites indicative of organic acidemias or fatty acid oxidation disorders detected.

results.male_result: HPLC is an analytical technique. Normal results for infants differ significantly from adults, particularly regarding Hemoglobin analysis and metabolic markers. Common applications include: Hemoglobin Analysis (Thalassemia/Sickle Cell screening): Fetal Hemoglobin (HbF) is dominant at birth (50-85%) and decreases to <2% by 12 months; Adult Hemoglobin (HbA) increases reciprocally. Plasma Amino Acids: Used to detect inborn errors of metabolism; reference ranges are age-specific and distinct from adults due to rapid growth. Catecholamines: Ranges are generally higher in infants than adults. Vitamin D (25-OH): Target for sufficiency is typically >20-30 ng/mL, similar to adults but critical for rickets prevention.

Plasma Amino Acids: Used to detect inborn errors of metabolism; reference ranges are age-specific and distinct from adults due to rapid growth. results.male_result: Due to rapid somatic growth and active hematopoiesis, infants typically exhibit higher colony counts than adults. Approximate ranges per 10^5 mononuclear cells: CFU-GM (Granulocyte-Macrophage): 60-300 colonies; CFU-E (Erythroid): 150-600 colonies; BFU-E (Burst-forming unit-Erythroid): 40-120 colonies; CFU-GEMM (Mixed): 10-40 colonies. Colony growth is usually robust with normal morphology.

results.male_result: Epidermis: Stratified squamous epithelium is present but generally thinner than in adults; stratum corneum is thinner. The dermal-epidermal junction is flatter with less developed rete ridges. Dermis: Collagen fibers are finer, less dense, and more loosely arranged than in adults; elastic fibers are immature and sparse. Adnexal structures: Sebaceous glands may appear hyperplastic/prominent in neonates (due to maternal hormones) but typically become small and quiescent during the remainder of infancy. Eccrine glands are present. No fungal elements or pathogenic microorganisms identified. Demodex mites are typically absent (colonization is rare in infants).
